title: John O' The Side
content:
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'He is weil kend, Johne of the Syde,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻A greater theif did never ryde.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Sir Richard Maitland.


혻혻혻혻The Text is from the Percy Folio, but is given in modernised spelling. It lacks the beginning, probably, and one line in st. 3, which can be easily guessed; but as a whole it is an infinitely fresher and better ballad than that inserted in the Minstrelsy of Sir Walter Scott.


혻혻혻혻The Story is akin to that of Kinmont Willie (p. 49). John of the Side (on the river Liddel, nearly opposite Mangerton) first appears about 1550 in a list of freebooters against whom complaints were laid before the Bishop of Carlisle. He was, it seems, another of the Armstrong family.

혻혻혻혻Hobby Noble has a ballad[1] to himself (as the hero of the present ballad deserves), in which mention is made of Peter of Whitfield. This is doubtless the person mentioned in the first line of John o' the Side as having been killed presumably by John himself.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻[Footnote 1: Child, No. 189, from Caw's Poetical Museum, but not
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻of sufficient merit to be included here.]

혻혻혻혻'Culertun,' 10.1, is Chollerton on the Tyne. Percy suggests Challerton, and in the ballads upon which Scott founded his version the name is 'Choler-ford.' 'Howbrame wood' and 'Lord Clough' are not identified; and Flanders files, effective as they appear to be, are not otherwise known.

혻혻혻혻'The ballad,' says Professor Child, 'is one of the best in the world, and enough to make a horse-trooper of any young borderer, had he lacked the impulse.'


혻혻혻혻JOHN O' THE SIDE

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻1.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Peter o' Whifield he hath slain,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And John o' Side, he is ta'en,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And John is bound both hand and foot,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And to the New-castle he is gone.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻2.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But tidings came to the Sybil o' the Side,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻By the water-side as she ran;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻She took her kirtle by the hem,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And fast she run to Mangerton.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻3.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻...혻혻혻혻 ...혻혻혻혻 ...
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The lord was set down at his meat;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻When these tidings she did him tell,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Never a morsel might he eat.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻4.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But lords they wrung their fingers white,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Ladies did pull themselves by the hair,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Crying 'Alas and welladay!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻For John o' the Side we shall never see more.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻5.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'But we'll go sell our droves of kine,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And after them our oxen sell,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And after them our troops of sheep,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But we will loose him out of the New Castell.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻6.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But then bespake him Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And spoke these words wondrous high;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Says, 'Give me five men to myself,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And I'll fetch John o' the Side to thee.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻7.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Yea, thou'st have five, Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Of the best that are in this country;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻I'll give thee five thousand, Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻That walk in Tyvidale truly.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻8.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Nay, I'll have but five,' says Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'That shall walk away with me;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻We will ride like no men of war,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But like poor badgers we will be.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻9.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻They stuffed up all their bags with straw,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And their steeds barefoot must be;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Come on, my brethren,' says Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Come on your ways, and go with me.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻10.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And when they came to Culerton ford,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The water was up, they could it not go;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And then they were ware of a good old man,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻How his boy and he were at the plough.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻11.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'But stand you still,' says Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Stand you still here at this shore,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And I will ride to yonder old man,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And see where the gate it lies o'er.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻12.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'But Christ you save, father!' quoth he,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Christ both you save and see!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Where is the way over this ford?
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻For Christ's sake tell it me.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻13.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'But I have dwelled here three score year,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻So have I done three score and three;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻I never saw man nor horse go o'er,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Except it were a horse of tree.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻14.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'But fare thou well, thou good old man!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The devil in hell I leave with thee,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻No better comfort here this night
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Thou gives my brethren here and me.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻15.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But when he came to his brether again,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And told this tidings full of woe,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And then they found a well good gate
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻They might ride o'er by two and two.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻16.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And when they were come over the ford,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻All safe gotten at the last,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Thanks be to God!' says Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'The worst of our peril is past.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻17.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And then they came into Howbrame wood,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And there then they found a tree,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And cut it down then by the root.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The length was thirty foot and three.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻18.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And four of them did take the plank,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻As light as it had been a flea,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And carried it to the New Castle,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Where as John o' Side did lie.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻19.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And some did climb up by the walls,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And some did climb up by the tree,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Until they came up to the top of the castle,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Where John made his moan truly.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻20.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻He said, 'God be with thee, Sybil o' the Side!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻My own mother thou art,' quoth he;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'If thou knew this night I were here,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻A woe woman then wouldst thou be.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻21.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'And fare you well, Lord Mangerton!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And ever I say God be with thee!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻For if you knew this night I were here,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻You would sell your land for to loose me.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻22.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'And fare thou well, Much, Miller's son!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Much, Miller's son, I say;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Thou has been better at mirk midnight
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Than ever thou was at noon o' the day.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻23.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'And fare thou well, my good lord Clough!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Thou art thy father's son and heir;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Thou never saw him in all thy life
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But with him durst thou break a spear.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻24.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'We are brothers childer nine or ten,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And sisters children ten or eleven;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻We never came to the field to fight,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But the worst of us was counted a man.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻25.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But then bespake him Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And spake these words unto him;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Says 'Sleepest thou, wakest thou, John o' the Side,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Or art thou this castle within?'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻26.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'But who is there,' quoth John o' the Side,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'That knows my name so right and free?'
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'I am a bastard-brother of thine;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻This night I am comen for to loose thee.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻27.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Now nay, now nay,' quoth John o' the Side,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'It fears me sore that will not be,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻For a peck of gold and silver,' John said,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'In faith this night will not loose me.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻28.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But then bespake him Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And till his brother thus said he;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Says 'Four shall take this matter in hand,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And two shall tent our geldings free.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻29.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Four did break one door without,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Then John brake five himsel';
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But when they came to the iron door,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻It smote twelve upon the bell.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻30.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'It fears me sore,' said Much, the Miller,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'That here taken we all shall be;'
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'But go away, brethren,' said John o' the Side,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'For ever alas! this will not be.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻31.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'But fie upon thee!' said Hobby Noble;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Much, the Miller, fie upon thee!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻It sore fears me,' said Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Man that thou wilt never be.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻32.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But then he had Flanders files two or thee,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And he filed down that iron door,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And took John out of the New Castle,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And said 'Look thou never come here more!'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻33.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻When he had him forth of the New Castle,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Away with me, John, thou shalt ride.'
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But ever alas! it could not be,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻For John could neither sit nor stride.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻34.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But then he had sheets two or three,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And bound John's bolts fast to his feet,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And set him on a well good steed,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Himself on another by him set.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻35.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Then Hobby Noble smiled and lough,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And spoke these words in mickle pride;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Thou sits so finely on thy gelding
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻That, John, thou rides like a bride.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻36.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And when they came thorough Howbrame town,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻John's horse there stumbled at a stone;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Out and alas!' cried Much, the Miller,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'John, thou'll make us all be ta'en.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻37.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'But fie upon thee!' says Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Much, the Miller, fie on thee!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻I know full well,' says Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Man that thou wilt never be.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻38.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And when they came into Howbrame wood,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻He had Flanders files two or three
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻To file John's bolts beside his feet,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻That he might ride more easily.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻39.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Says 'John, now leap over a steed!'
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And John then he lope over five.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'I know well,' says Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'John, thy fellow is not alive.'

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻40.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Then he brought him home to Mangerton;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The lord then he was at his meat;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But when John o' the Side he there did see,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻For fain he could no more eat.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻41.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻He says 'Blest be thou, Hobby Noble,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻That ever thou wast man born!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Thou hast fetched us home good John o' the Side,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻That was now clean from us gone.'
meaning:
This evocative piece by Frank Sidgwick, titled "John O' The Side", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
